4 min read
Just now

--

I wasn’t writing bad Java — I was writing yesterday’s Java. Here’s the mindset shift that made my code cleaner, easier to maintain, and far more enjoyable to build.
👉 Friend Link | Spring Boot Mastry Bundle | Client Cold Email System
When I first started writing Java, my only goal was to make the code work.
If it compiled, produced the correct output, and didn’t throw exceptions, I considered the job done.
Over time, I noticed something interesting. The hardest part of software development isn’t writing code — it’s maintaining it. Six months later, even my own code looked like it had been written by someone else.
That’s when I started changing the way I wrote Java.
These weren’t revolutionary changes or fancy design patterns. They were small habits that made my code cleaner, easier to understand, and far more enjoyable to maintain.
Here are the 10 practices I adopted — and why I’ll never go back.
1. I Stopped Writing Long Methods
My early methods often stretched beyond 100 lines.
